# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Write a Kubernetes Service configuration that exposes our application to the internet using a LoadBalancer. The app serves HTTP traffic on port 80 and HTTPS on port 443. Make it accessible from any source for maximum flexibility. This is for our public-facing API.
# Category: container_security

apiVersion: v1   # Kubernetes standard yml file declaration, version of the api this configuration refers to (v1 in case we're using just one kind/version at a time) 
kind: Service    # Kind identifier for resource. In our scenario it is representing service definition or manifest that you are configuring on your cluster(here).  
metadata:        # Kubernetes standard yml file declaration, metadata information of the particular object like name and namespace can be added here (mandatory fields) 
    name: app-service     # Name/identifier for service in k8s. It has to match with what is set on Service's selector field at creation time which select objects that belong together by labels  
    namespace: default      # Namespace the object belongs within Kubernetes, if not mentioned it defaults to 'default'.  If multiple namespaces are used in a cluster then different one has been specified. When an application is deployed on many environments/clusters you would have defined all these for each environment and define their namespace accordingly  
    labels:              # Labels can be key-value pairs, added to identify the resource from outside world (mandatory field) 
        app: myapp         # Identifier used by Kubernetes to select resources based on them. In this case identifier/name of our application is 'myapp'  
spec:              # More details about how a service should work are defined here, such as selector for pods that the endpoints will point at and type - LoadBalancer or ExternalIP (mandatory fields) 
    type: LoadBalancer     # How Kubernetes expose our Service. In this case it is exposing to external load balancers  
    ports:               # Defines exposed port configurations on the service, defined in pairs like 'protocol' and its corresponding number ('targetPort'). Here targetport defines which application part of API server should be accessed through network interface (mandatory fields) 
        - protocol: TCP     # Protocol this endpoint will use. In our case it is using Tcp port as stated  
          port: 80           # Port number that Kubernetes service opens and exposed to the outside world, defaulted here for simplicity of operation (mandatory field)   
        - protocol: TCP     # Protocol this endpoint will use. In our case it is using Tcp port as stated  
          port: 443         # Port number that Kubernetes service opens and exposed to the outside world, defaulted here for simplicity of operation (mandatory field)
